From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

**Boikoo tarkbei** or **Daur hockey** is a game similar to [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey") or [street hockey](/wiki/Street_hockey "Street hockey"). It has been played for about 1,000 years[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1) by the [Daur](/wiki/Daur_people "Daur people") people, an ethnic group from [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia"), China.[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1)

The game involves teams of men playing a ball-like knob of [apricot](/wiki/Apricot "Apricot") root with long wooden branches.[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1) At night, the game is played with a [felt](/wiki/Felt "Felt")-covered ball that is set alight.[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1) A game is reported to last for two periods of roughly 15 minutes.[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1)

**Poolie tarkbei** is the Daur version of [polo](/wiki/Polo "Polo"). Boikoo and poolie are two popular game among Daurs.

Some Daurs believe the [Daur](/wiki/Daur_language "Daur language") term *boikoo* and *poolie* was originated from Chinese term *buqiu* ([budaqiu](/w/index.php?title=Budaqiu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Budaqiu (page does not exist)") or buqiu was a popular game in [Tang China](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty")) and Tibetan term *polon*, and deny the Khitan origin of Daurs. Others believe the Daur boikoo was originated from the **Khitan hockey**.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

When Western field hockey was introduced to China, it was taken up enthusiastically by the Daurs, whose affinity for the game has helped China to improve its global standing in field hockey.[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1) At the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2008_Summer_Olympics "2008 Summer Olympics"), a third of the [Chinese men's field hockey team](/wiki/China_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "China men's national field hockey team") was from [Morin Dawa](/wiki/Morin_Dawa "Morin Dawa"), a city in the [Morin Dawa Daur Autonomous Banner](/wiki/Morin_Dawa_Daur_Autonomous_Banner "Morin Dawa Daur Autonomous Banner") in Inner Mongolia.[[1]](#cite_note-nyt-1)
